analogy: Think of "Geography as a Discipline" through the chapter's own opening images: GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE This unit deals with • Geography as an integrating discipline; as a science of spatial attributes • Branches of geography; importance of physical geography UNIT I You have studied geography as one of the components of your Social Science course upto... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE, GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE 3, GEOGRAPHY AS AN INTEGRATING DISCIPLINE, GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE 5. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Geography as a Discipline is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that gEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE This unit deals with • Geography as an integrating discipline; as a science of spatial attributes • Branches of geography; importance of physical geography UNIT I

The text explains that you have studied geography as one of the components of your Social Science course upto the secondary stage.

The text explains that you are already aware of some of the phenomena of geographical nature in the world and its different parts.

The text explains that now, you will study ‘Geography’ as an independent subject and learn about the physical environment of the earth, human activities and their interactive relationships.

The text explains that therefore, a pertinent question you can ask at this stage is — Why should we study geography?

The text explains that our lives are affected by our surroundings in many ways.

The text explains that we depend on the resources to sustain ourselves in the surrounding areas.

The text explains that primitive societies subsisted on ‘natural means of subsistence’, i.e. edible plants and animals.

The text explains that with the passage of time, we developed technologies and started producing our food using natural resources such as land, soil and water.

The text explains that we adjusted our food habits and clothing according to the prevailing weather conditions.

The text explains that there are variations in the natural resource base, technological development, adaptation with and modification of physical environment, social organisations and cultural development.

The text explains that as a student of geography, you should be curious to know about all the phenomena which vary over space.

The text explains that you learn about the diverse lands and people.

The text explains that you should also be interested in understanding the changes which have taken place over time.

The text explains that geography equips you to appreciate diversity and investigate into the causes responsible for creating such variations over time and space.

The text explains that you will develop skills to understand the globe converted into maps and have a visual sense of the earth’s surface.

The text explains that now the next question which you may like to ask is — What is geography?

The text explains that it is also the home of many other creatures, big and small, which live on the earth and sustain.

The text explains that there are variations in its social and cultural features too.

The text explains that this variation provides a clue to the understanding of the relationship between the physical environment and social/cultural features.

The text explains that the physical environment has provided the stage, on which human societies enacted the drama of their creative skills with the tools and techniques which they invented and evolved in the process of their cultural development.

The text explains that now, you should be able to attempt the answer of the question posed earlier as to “What is geography”?

The text explains that in very simple words, it can be said that geography is the description of the earth.

The text explains that the word has been derived from two roots from Greek language geo (earth) and graphos (description).

The text explains that gEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE 3 Put together, they mean description of the earth.

The text explains that the earth has always been seen as the abode of human beings and thus, scholars defined geography as, “the description of the earth as the abode of human beings”.

The text explains that you are aware of the fact that reality is always multifaceted and the ‘earth’ is also multi-dimensional, that is why many disciplines from natural sciences such as geology, pedology, oceanography, botany, zoology and meteorology and a number of sister disciplines in social sciences such as economics, history, sociology, political science, anthropology, etc. study different aspects of the earth’s surface.

The text explains that we have noted that there exist variations over the surface of the earth in its physical as well as cultural environment.

The text explains that geographers do not study only the variations in the phenomena over the earth’s surface (space) but also study the associations with the other factors which cause these variations.

The text explains that a geographer explains the phenomena in a frame of cause and effect relationship, as it does not only help in interpretation but also foresees the phenomena in future.

The text explains that they change over time as a result of the interactive processes between ever changing earth and untiring and ever-active human beings.

The text explains that primitive human societies were directly dependent on their immediate environment.

The text explains that as you already know, the present society has passed the stage of primitive societies, which were directly dependent on their immediate physical environment for sustenance.

The text explains that present societies have modified their natural environment by inventing and using technology and thus, have expanded the horizon of their operation by appropriating and utilising the resources provided by nature.

The text explains that human beings have claimed their contribution using natural resources.

The text explains that geography as a discipline is concerned with three sets of questions: (i) Some questions are related to the identification of the patterns of natural and cultural features as found over the surface of the earth.

The text explains that (ii) Some questions are related to the distribution of the natural and human/ cultural features over the surface of the earth.

The text explains that taken together, both these questions take care of distributional and locational aspects of the natural and cultural features.

The text explains that these questions provided inventorised information of what features and where located.

The text explains that these two questions did not make geography a scientific discipline till the third question was added.

The text explains that (iii) The third question is related to the explanation or the causal relationships between features and the processes and phenomena.

The text explains that this aspect of geography is related to the question, why?

The text explains that it attempts spatial synthesis, and history attempts temporal synthesis.

The text explains that it recognises the fact that the world is a system of interdependencies.

The text explains that spatial distance itself has been a very potent factor to alter the course of history of the world.

The text explains that the geographical factors have modified the course of history in different parts of the world.

The text explains that can you convert time into space and vice versa?

The text explains that (i) You observe every day in your surroundings that there is variation in natural as well as cultural phenomena.

The text explains that all the birds and animals you see, are different.

The text explains that all these different elements are found on the earth.

The text explains that can you now argue that geography is the study of “areal differentiation”?

The text explains that (ii) You have already studied geography, history, civics and economics as parts of social studies.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 1: Geography as a Discipline

cases.point: In India, Himalayas have acted as great barriers and provided protection but the passes provided routes to the migrants and invaders from Central Asia. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch1 pg 3)

cases.name: The
cases.point: The sea coast has encouraged contact with people from East and Southeast Asia, Europe and Africa. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch1 pg 3)

cases.name: Navigation
cases.point: Navigation technology helped European countries to colonise a number of countries of Asia and Africa, including India as they got accessibility (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch1 pg 3)

schemes.name: As
schemes.point: As a social science discipline, geography studies the ‘spatial organisation’ and ‘spatial integration’. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch1 pg 3)

prelims_traps: Do not treat GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE and GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE 3 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Geography as a Discipline; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Geography as a Discipline through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE and GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE 3 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
